[Southwestern Central](/wiki/Southwestern_Central_High_School "Southwestern Central High School") ([Jamestown, New York](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"))

([/ˌsɪriˈɑːni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SEE-ree-AH-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); born June 15, 1981) is an American professional [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") coach who is the [head coach](/wiki/Head_coach "Head coach") for the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL). He previously served as an assistant coach with the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") from 2009 to 2012, as an assistant coach with the [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") / [Los Angeles Chargers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chargers "Los Angeles Chargers") from 2013 to 2017, then as the [offensive coordinator](/wiki/Offensive_coordinator "Offensive coordinator") for the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") from 2018 to 2020.

**Nicholas John Sirianni** ([/ˌsɪriˈɑːni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SEE-ree-AH-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); born June 15, 1981) is an American professional [football](/wiki/American_football "American football") coach who is the [head coach](/wiki/Head_coach "Head coach") for the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL). He previously served as an assistant coach with the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") from 2009 to 2012, as an assistant coach with the [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") / [Los Angeles Chargers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chargers "Los Angeles Chargers") from 2013 to 2017, then as the [offensive coordinator](/wiki/Offensive_coordinator "Offensive coordinator") for the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") from 2018 to 2020.

Since his hiring as head coach for the Eagles, Sirianni has led them to the playoffs in each of his first five seasons as head coach, including winning three division titles, two conference championships, and a victory in [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX") in 2025.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Sirianni was born on June 15, 1981, in [Jamestown, New York](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"), the son of Fran and Amy Sirianni. Fran was a middle school science teacher and the former head football coach at [Southwestern Central High School](/wiki/Southwestern_Central_High_School "Southwestern Central High School") in [West Ellicott, New York](/wiki/West_Ellicott,_New_York "West Ellicott, New York"), where Nick graduated in 1999.[[1]](#cite_note-1) He is of Italian descent through his father with roots in [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Sirianni was raised [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and grew up a fan of the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers"), owing to his family having roots in the Pittsburgh area.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Sirianni played wide receiver at [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") [Mount Union](/wiki/Mount_Union_Purple_Raiders_football "Mount Union Purple Raiders football") in [Alliance, Ohio](/wiki/Alliance,_Ohio "Alliance, Ohio"), winning national championships in 2000, 2001, and 2002.[[5]](#cite_note-berman1-5) Though a calf injury and [compartment syndrome](/wiki/Compartment_syndrome "Compartment syndrome") nearly ended his playing career as a sophomore,[[6]](#cite_note-6) Sirianni started for three years.[[5]](#cite_note-berman1-5) As a senior in 2003, he had 998 yards and 13 touchdowns[[7]](#cite_note-Frank-2021-7) and graduated with a degree in education.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Sirianni played one season of professional football for the [Canton Legends](/wiki/Canton_Legends "Canton Legends") of the [American Indoor Football League](/wiki/American_Indoor_Football "American Indoor Football").[[7]](#cite_note-Frank-2021-7)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### College coaching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: College coaching")]

Sirianni began coaching as the defensive backs coach at [Mount Union](/wiki/Mount_Union_Purple_Raiders_football "Mount Union Purple Raiders football"), his alma mater.[[9]](#cite_note-9) After one season of coaching Mount Union, Sirianni was hired by [Indiana University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/IUP_Crimson_Hawks "IUP Crimson Hawks") in [Indiana, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Indiana,_Pennsylvania "Indiana, Pennsylvania"), where he coached wide receivers for three years.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Kansas City Chiefs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Kansas City Chiefs")]

In [2009](/wiki/2009_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2009 Kansas City Chiefs season"), Sirianni was hired as offensive quality control coach for the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") by [Todd Haley](/wiki/Todd_Haley "Todd Haley"), the new head coach of the Chiefs.[[7]](#cite_note-Frank-2021-7) Sirianni and Haley got to know each other when they attended the same [YMCA](/wiki/YMCA "YMCA") when Sirianni was in college and Haley was wide receivers coach for the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Sirianni was retained under new coach [Romeo Crennel](/wiki/Romeo_Crennel "Romeo Crennel") and was promoted to wide receivers coach in Crennel's only season as the Chiefs head coach. Sirianni was not retained for the 2013 season under new head coach [Andy Reid](/wiki/Andy_Reid "Andy Reid").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### San Diego Chargers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: San Diego Chargers")]

Sirianni joined the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") when [Mike McCoy](/wiki/Mike_McCoy_(American_football_coach) "Mike McCoy (American football coach)") was hired as the team's head coach in 2013.[[14]](#cite_note-chargers-14) The following season, Sirianni became the team's quarterbacks coach, working with quarterback [Philip Rivers](/wiki/Philip_Rivers "Philip Rivers") and offensive coordinator [Frank Reich](/wiki/Frank_Reich "Frank Reich").[[5]](#cite_note-berman1-5) In 2016, Sirianni became the wide receivers coach.[[14]](#cite_note-chargers-14)

### Indianapolis Colts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Indianapolis Colts")]

After Reich became the head coach of the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") in 2018, he hired Sirianni as offensive coordinator.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Sirianni developed a close relationship with Reich, though unlike some head coaches, Reich chose to [call the team's plays](/wiki/Play_calling_system "Play calling system") rather than delegate the responsibility to Sirianni.[[16]](#cite_note-16) During his three years as offensive coordinator with the Colts, Sirianni had a different starting quarterback each year, working with [Andrew Luck](/wiki/Andrew_Luck "Andrew Luck"), [Jacoby Brissett](/wiki/Jacoby_Brissett "Jacoby Brissett"), and [Philip Rivers](/wiki/Philip_Rivers "Philip Rivers"). The Colts made the playoffs twice and finished 10th, 19th, and 12th in offensive [DVOA](/wiki/Football_Outsiders#DVOA "Football Outsiders"), a measure of offensive success.[[5]](#cite_note-berman1-5)

### Philadelphia Eagles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Philadelphia Eagles")]

#### 2021 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2021 season")]

See also: [2021 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/2021_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2021 Philadelphia Eagles season")

On January 24, 2021, Sirianni was hired to become the head coach of the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") after the firing of [Doug Pederson](/wiki/Doug_Pederson "Doug Pederson").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) Two months later, the Eagles traded quarterback [Carson Wentz](/wiki/Carson_Wentz "Carson Wentz") to the Colts, leaving 2020 second-round pick [Jalen Hurts](/wiki/Jalen_Hurts "Jalen Hurts") as the presumed starter.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Sirianni put together a staff of young coaches, including defensive coordinator [Jonathan Gannon](/wiki/Jonathan_Gannon "Jonathan Gannon") and offensive coordinator [Shane Steichen](/wiki/Shane_Steichen "Shane Steichen"), both of whom had previously worked with Sirianni. Although much of Pederson's staff was replaced, Sirianni retained veteran offensive line coach [Jeff Stoutland](/wiki/Jeff_Stoutland "Jeff Stoutland").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

On September 12, 2021, Sirianni made his regular-season head coaching debut against the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2021_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2021 Atlanta Falcons season") and led the Eagles to a 32–6 victory.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Despite a 2–5 start, Sirianni finished his first season as head coach with a 9–8 record and a wild card berth.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The Eagles lost in the [Wild Card Round](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_31,_Philadelphia_Eagles_15 "2021–22 NFL playoffs") of the playoffs to the defending Super Bowl champion [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2021_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2021 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") by a score 31–15.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Sirianni was the only first-year coach to lead a team to the playoffs in the 2021 NFL season,[[24]](#cite_note-24) and the third Eagles head coach to make the playoffs in their first year as head coach, joining [Chip Kelly](/wiki/Chip_Kelly "Chip Kelly") in [2013](/wiki/2013_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2013 Philadelphia Eagles season") and [Ray Rhodes](/wiki/Ray_Rhodes "Ray Rhodes") in [1995](/wiki/1995_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "1995 Philadelphia Eagles season").[[25]](#cite_note-x733-25)

#### 2022 season: Super Bowl LVII run

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2022 season: Super Bowl LVII run")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Nick_Sirianni_2022_%28cropped%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Nick_Sirianni_2022_%28cropped%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nick_Sirianni_2022_(cropped)_(cropped).jpg)

Sirianni in 2022

See also: [2022 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/2022_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2022 Philadelphia Eagles season")

The Eagles compiled a 14–3 record in the regular season, earning the [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") division championship and a first-round bye in the playoffs.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The Eagles became the first team since the [1989 Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/1989_Minnesota_Vikings "1989 Minnesota Vikings") to record at least 70 sacks,[[27]](#cite_note-27) led the league in fewest passing yards allowed, and set a franchise record for wins and points scored in a season.[[28]](#cite_note-28) One notable sideline incident occurred during a Week 11 away matchup against the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2022_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2022 Indianapolis Colts season"), where Sirianni had previously served as an offensive coordinator. After the Eagles narrowly defeated the Colts 17–16, Sirianni turned to a group of Eagles fans that had travelled to [Lucas Oil Stadium](/wiki/Lucas_Oil_Stadium "Lucas Oil Stadium") and shouted that the win "was for [Frank Reich](/wiki/Frank_Reich "Frank Reich")", the coach who mentored him and who was fired as head coach of the Colts earlier that month.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

During the postseason, Sirianni led the Eagles to their fourth Super Bowl in franchise history after a 38–7 [Divisional Round](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Philadelphia_Eagles_38,_New_York_Giants_7 "2022–23 NFL playoffs") playoff victory over the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") and a 31–7 [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_NFL_playoffs#NFC_Championship_Game:_Philadelphia_Eagles_31,_San_Francisco_49ers_7 "2022–23 NFL playoffs") victory over the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers").[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The Eagles lost to the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2022_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2022 Kansas City Chiefs season") in [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII") by a score of 38–35.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

#### 2023 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2023 season")]

See also: [2023 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/2023_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2023 Philadelphia Eagles season")

During the offseason, Sirianni lost both offensive coordinator [Shane Steichen](/wiki/Shane_Steichen "Shane Steichen") and defensive coordinator [Jonathan Gannon](/wiki/Jonathan_Gannon "Jonathan Gannon"), both of whom received head coaching jobs with the Indianapolis Colts and Arizona Cardinals, respectively.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

The Eagles once again compiled a 10–1 regular season record to start the season, but experienced a late season slide and finished with a 1–5 record and ultimately lost out on the NFC East Division title and the #2-seed in the NFC to the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") in the last week of the season.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Sirianni became the first head coach in NFL history to experience a 1–5 record to end the season and make the playoffs. The Eagles were ultimately eliminated by the Buccaneers in the [Wild Card Round](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_32,_Philadelphia_Eagles_9 "2023–24 NFL playoffs") of the 2023 NFL Playoffs.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Sirianni was widely blamed for both the offensive and defensive regression of the Eagles in 2023.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In particular, Sirianni caused a significant controversy after removing defensive coordinator [Sean Desai](/wiki/Sean_Desai "Sean Desai") from defensive play calling and passed them onto former New England Patriots defensive coordinator and Detroit Lions head coach [Matt Patricia](/wiki/Matt_Patricia "Matt Patricia"), a move that ultimately backfired and led the Eagles to finish second to last in total defense.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) Sirianni ultimately fired Desai, Patricia, and offensive coordinator [Brian Johnson](/wiki/Brian_Johnson_(American_football_coach) "Brian Johnson (American football coach)") after the 2023 season.[[40]](#cite_note-y356-40)[[41]](#cite_note-a048-41)

#### 2024 season: Super Bowl LIX victory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2024 season: Super Bowl LIX victory")]

See also: [2024 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/2024_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2024 Philadelphia Eagles season")

During Week 2 against the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2024_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2024 Atlanta Falcons season"), Sirianni was widely criticized for throwing the ball on 3rd and 3 at the Atlanta 10-yard line up 18–15, where the pass fell incomplete, giving Atlanta time to march down the field and secure the victory.[[42]](#cite_note-g358-42) The following week, despite a 15–12 victory over the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2024_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2024 New Orleans Saints season"), Sirianni still received criticism for his fourth down aggression.[[43]](#cite_note-43) After a Week 6 home victory over the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/2024_Cleveland_Browns_season "2024 Cleveland Browns season"), Sirianni drew criticism for having a shouting match against the Eagles' fans in attendance behind the team's sideline.[[44]](#cite_note-Frank-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) While the Eagles were on the field in victory formation, TV cameras showed that Sirianni walked behind the bench, put an index finger to his right ear then shouted words that could not be heard from afar[[46]](#cite_note-46) but were interpreted as, "I can't hear you."[[44]](#cite_note-Frank-44)[[47]](#cite_note-47) Sirianni refused to go into any detail on what precipitated his post-game antics.[[44]](#cite_note-Frank-44)

Despite early struggles and a 2–2 start, Sirianni coached the Eagles to a 14–3 record and an NFC East title, finishing as the #2-seed in the NFC.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-i398-49) Propelled by the addition of running back [Saquon Barkley](/wiki/Saquon_Barkley "Saquon Barkley") and an astounding defensive turnaround from the prior season under the leadership of defensive coordinator [Vic Fangio](/wiki/Vic_Fangio "Vic Fangio"), the Eagles secured a [Wild Card Round](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Philadelphia_Eagles_22,_Green_Bay_Packers_10 "2024–25 NFL playoffs") victory over the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") and a [Divisional Round](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_Philadelphia_Eagles_28,_Los_Angeles_Rams_22 "2024–25 NFL playoffs") victory over the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams").[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-s591-51)[[52]](#cite_note-e287-52) For the second time in his career, Sirianni won the [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_NFL_playoffs#NFC_Championship:_Philadelphia_Eagles_55,_Washington_Commanders_23 "2024–25 NFL playoffs"), this time against the [Washington Commanders](/wiki/Washington_Commanders "Washington Commanders") 55–23, in which the Eagles broke an NFL record for the most points ever scored in a conference championship game en route to [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX").[[53]](#cite_note-l402-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In a Super Bowl rematch against the Chiefs, the Eagles won 40–22, giving Sirianni his first Super Bowl title as a head coach.[[55]](#cite_note-a252-55) Closing out the 2024 season, Sirianni bested a 70% win percentage over his first four seasons as an NFL head coach with the Eagles, the highest career win percentage of any active NFL head coach at the time.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

#### 2025 season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2025 season")]

See also: [2025 Philadelphia Eagles season](/wiki/2025_Philadelphia_Eagles_season "2025 Philadelphia Eagles season")

On May 19, 2025, Sirianni and the Eagles agreed to a multi-year contract extension.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

After Week 17, Sirianni controversially decided to rest his starters in the regular season finale against the Commanders despite having a path to the #2-seed with a win and a [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears") loss.[[58]](#cite_note-58) However, the Eagles lost to the Commanders by a score of 24–17 and earned the #3-seed in the NFC with an 11–6 record.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) The Eagles would’ve been the #2 seed with a win, as the Bears lost to the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions").[[61]](#cite_note-61) During the [Wild Card Round](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_NFL_playoffs#NFC:_San_Francisco_49ers_23,_Philadelphia_Eagles_19 "2025–26 NFL playoffs") against the 49ers, the Eagles lost 23–19, ending their hopes for a repeat.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Additionally, at the end of the game, Sirianni took a timeout before a fourth and 11 that fell incomplete, sealing the game. Had Sirianni had the timeout, he would’ve had one more chance to get the ball back and score. That decision was criticized as well.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

## Coaching tree

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Coaching tree")]

Sirianni has served under seven head coaches:

* [Larry Kehres](/wiki/Larry_Kehres "Larry Kehres"), [Mount Union](/wiki/Mount_Union_Purple_Raiders_football "Mount Union Purple Raiders football") (2004–2005)
* [Lou Tepper](/wiki/Lou_Tepper "Lou Tepper"), [IUP](/wiki/IUP_Crimson_Hawks_football "IUP Crimson Hawks football") (2006–2008)
* [Todd Haley](/wiki/Todd_Haley "Todd Haley"), [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") (2009–2011)
* [Romeo Crennel](/wiki/Romeo_Crennel "Romeo Crennel"), Kansas City Chiefs (2011–2012)
* [Mike McCoy](/wiki/Mike_McCoy_(American_football_coach) "Mike McCoy (American football coach)"), [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") (2013–2016)
* [Anthony Lynn](/wiki/Anthony_Lynn "Anthony Lynn"), [Los Angeles Chargers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chargers "Los Angeles Chargers") (2017)
* [Frank Reich](/wiki/Frank_Reich "Frank Reich"), [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") (2018–2020)

Three of Sirianni's coaching assistants have become head coaches in the NFL:

* [Jonathan Gannon](/wiki/Jonathan_Gannon "Jonathan Gannon"), [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals") (2023–2025)[[64]](#cite_note-64)
* [Shane Steichen](/wiki/Shane_Steichen "Shane Steichen"), [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") (2023–present)[[65]](#cite_note-65)
* [Kellen Moore](/wiki/Kellen_Moore "Kellen Moore"), [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") (2025–present)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nick_Sirianni&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Sirianni is married to Brett Ashley Sirianni with whom he has three children; they met when Nick was working for the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs").[[67]](#cite_note-67) His father, Fran, and his brother, Jay, are both former head coaches of [Southwestern Central High School](/wiki/Southwestern_Central_High_School "Southwestern Central High School"), while his brother [Mike](/wiki/Mike_Sirianni "Mike Sirianni") is the head coach at [Washington & Jefferson College](/wiki/Washington_%26_Jefferson_Presidents_football "Washington & Jefferson Presidents football") in [Washington, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Washington,_Pennsylvania "Washington, Pennsylvania").[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)
